title: Reading and Use of English – Part 7
description: You are going to read an article about the history of the telescope. Choose from paragraphs A–G which fits each gap. There is one extra paragraph.

content.articleTitle: Eyes on the Universe: The Story of the Telescope
content.text: The invention of the telescope did not happen in a vacuum; it was the result of centuries of progress in glassmaking and optics. By the early 1600s, spectacle makers in the Netherlands were experimenting with combining lenses to magnify distant objects. In 1608, Hans Lippershey applied for a patent for a 'spyglass,' sparking a revolution that would shift our place in the cosmos. ||(41)|| [41]News of this Dutch invention traveled fast, reaching Italy by the following year[/41]. Galileo Galilei, a mathematics professor in Padua, was fascinated by the reports. Although he had never seen the device, he understood the principles behind it and set about building his own version, significantly improving the magnification. ||(42)|| [42]Unlike the Dutch, who saw the spyglass as a military tool for spotting enemy ships, Galileo turned his lenses toward the night sky[/42]. What he saw shattered the ancient Aristotelian view of a perfect, unchanging universe. He discovered mountains and craters on the Moon, four moons orbiting Jupiter, and the phases of Venus. ||(43)|| [43]However, these early refracting telescopes had a major limitation: chromatic aberration[/43]. Because lenses bend different colors of light by different amounts, images were often surrounded by a distracting rainbow-colored blur. This physical hurdle meant that to get a clearer image, telescopes had to be incredibly long—some stretching over 45 meters. ||(44)|| [44]This problem was finally solved by Isaac Newton in 1668[/44]. Instead of using a glass lens to gather light, Newton used a curved metal mirror. This 'reflecting telescope' eliminated the color blurring and allowed for a much more compact design, paving the way for the massive instruments used by modern astronomers. ||(45)|| [45]As the 20th century dawned, the limits of Earth-bound observation became clear[/45]. Even the most powerful telescopes were hindered by the Earth’s atmosphere, which distorts light and blocks certain wavelengths. The solution was to launch a telescope into the vacuum of space, where it could see the universe in crystal-clear detail. ||(46)|| [46]Today, the James Webb Space Telescope is pushing these boundaries even further, peering back to the very beginning of time[/46]. It is the direct descendant of Lippershey’s simple spyglass, a testament to our enduring need to look further, see clearer, and understand the vast darkness that surrounds our tiny planet.

paragraphs.B: Indeed, [41] this first patent application [/41] caused a stir among European scientists. While the patent was ultimately denied because the device was considered too easy to copy, the concept of 'distance-seeing' was now out in the open, and there was no going back.
paragraphs.A: This [42] technical mastery [/42] allowed him to produce a telescope that magnified objects thirty times. He quickly realized that this was not just a toy for the curious, but a scientific instrument that could challenge the very foundations of human knowledge.
paragraphs.E: These [43] revolutionary observations [/43] provided the first physical evidence that the Earth was not the center of all motion. It was a dangerous discovery that brought Galileo into direct conflict with the religious authorities of the time, who viewed his findings as heretical.
paragraphs.F: The [44] impracticality of these long tubes [/44] led to a period of stagnation in optical design. Astronomers struggled to keep the lenses aligned, and the slightest vibration could ruin an entire night of observation, making the search for a new method a matter of urgency.
paragraphs.C: This [45] shift from glass to mirrors [/45] marked the birth of modern astrophysics. It allowed for the construction of telescopes with much larger apertures, capable of capturing the faint light from distant galaxies that had previously been invisible to the human eye.
paragraphs.G: This [46] leap into the cosmos [/46] was achieved with the launch of the Hubble Space Telescope in 1990. Despite an initial flaw in its mirror, subsequent repair missions turned it into one of the most productive scientific instruments in history, revealing the age and expansion of the universe.
paragraphs.D: Many [47] amateur stargazers [/47] still prefer the classic look of a refracting telescope. They argue that for viewing planets within our own solar system, the high contrast and sharpness provided by glass lenses still offer an experience that mirrors cannot quite match.

questions.explanation: B refers to the 'patent application' mentioned before the gap and explains why it was denied, linking to the spread of the invention.
A links Galileo's 'technical mastery' and improved magnification to the realization that it was a 'scientific instrument'.
E refers back to the 'revolutionary observations' (Jupiter's moons, etc.) and explains the resulting conflict with religious authorities.
F explains the 'impracticality' of the extremely long telescopes mentioned before the gap, setting up the need for Newton's reflecting design.
C describes the 'shift from glass to mirrors' initiated by Newton and its impact on the ability to see distant galaxies.
G identifies the 'leap into the cosmos' as the launch of the Hubble Space Telescope, following the discussion of atmospheric distortion.
